per_question.question: What information is stored about products in the manufacturing system?
per_question.expected_answer: Products are tracked with a unique identifier (product_id), descriptive name (product_name), classification as finished good, assembly, or component (product_type), optional parent product reference for hierarchical structures (parent_product_id), standard unit cost (base_cost), typical procurement or production lead time (lead_time_days), and active status flag (is_active).
per_question.generated_answer:
In the manufacturing system, information about products is stored in the **`product`** table. Each row represents a manufactured item (finished goods, assemblies, and components) identified by **`product_id`** (VARCHAR(20), primary key).

The table stores these product attributes: **`product_name`** (VARCHAR(100), NOT NULL), **`product_type`** (classified as `FINISHED_GOOD`, `ASSEMBLY`, or `COMPONENT`; NOT NULL with default `'FINISHED_GOOD'`), **`parent_product_id`** (VARCHAR(20), a self-referencing foreign key to `product(product_id)` to represent hierarchical assemblies), **`base_cost`** (DECIMAL(10,2), NOT NULL with default 0.00 and CHECK constraint `>= 0`), **`lead_time_days`** (INT, NOT NULL with default 0 and CHECK constraint `>= 0`), and **`is_active`** (yes_no_t, NOT NULL with default `'Y'` as the active status flag).

The schema also supports multi-level product structure via the self-referential **`parent_product_id` → product_id** relationship (including unlimited nesting depth as described), and it provides an index **`idx_product_parent`** on `parent_product_id` (where not null) to support hierarchical queries. Conceptually, products participate in the manufacturing workflow by appearing in **BOMs**, being produced via **work orders**, appearing in **shipments** (outbound), and being stored in **inventory** records.

# Manufacturing Business Glossary

This glossary defines the core business concepts and terminology used in the manufacturing supply chain domain. It covers product structures, materials management, production workflows, and quality assurance processes.

## Product

A Product represents any manufactured item in the system, including finished goods ready for sale, sub-assemblies used in production, and individual components. Products are organized in a hierarchical structure where complex products can contain simpler products as parts. Each product has a unique identifier, name, type classification, base cost, and lead time for procurement or production. Products can be active or inactive based on current manufacturing requirements.

**Key Attributes: ** - Unique product identifier (20 characters) - Product name and description - Type classification: FINISHED_GOOD, ASSEMBLY, or COMPONENT - Hierarchical relationship via parent_product_id - Base cost and lead time information - Active status flag **Relationships:** - A Product can have multiple child Products (via BOM hierarchy) - A Product can reference a parent Product (for multi-level assemblies) - Products are used in Work Orders for production - Products appear in Shipments (outbound) - Products are stored in Inventory records ## Component A Component represents atomic raw materials or purchased parts that cannot be further broken down into simpler manufactured items. Components are the building blocks used in manufacturing and are procured from external suppliers. Each component has a standard cost, unit of measure, and may be associated with technical specifications that define quality requirements. **Key Attributes:** - Unique component identifier - Component name and category classification - Unit of measure (EA, KG, M, L, etc.) - Standard cost for procurement - Technical specification reference **Relationships:** - Components are supplied by multiple Suppliers (many-to-many) - Components have Inventory records tracking stock levels - Components may reference Specifications for quality requirements ## BOM (Bill of Materials) The Bill of Materials (BOM) defines the composition of products by specifying which components and sub-assemblies are required to manufacture a parent product. Each BOM entry indicates a parent product, a component product (which can be either a component or sub-assembly), the required quantity, unit of measure, and hierarchical level in the product structure. BOM entries support recursive relationships to represent multi-level product assemblies. **Key Attributes:** - Unique BOM identifier - Parent product reference (what is being built) - Component product reference (what goes into it) - Required quantity and unit of measure - BOM level (depth in hierarchy) - Optional component flag **Relationships:** - BOM connects Products to other Products (parent-child) - Multiple BOM entries define a complete product structure - BOM structure is used for material requirements planning - BOM levels support hierarchical product explosions ## Supplier A Supplier is an external vendor or company that provides raw materials, components, or finished goods to the manufacturing organization. Suppliers are evaluated based on performance ratings, preferred status for strategic partnerships, and maintain contact information for procurement operations. Suppliers are associated with components they can provide through approved supplier relationships. **Key Attributes:** - Unique supplier identifier - Supplier name and contact details - Contact email and phone number - Performance rating (1.0 to 5.0 scale) - Preferred supplier flag **Relationships:** - Suppliers provide multiple Components (many-to-many via component_supplier) - Suppliers are linked to Inbound Shipments - Suppliers have negotiated pricing and lead times per component

# Manufacturing Data Dictionary

This data dictionary documents the database schema for the manufacturing supply chain system. It includes all tables, columns, data types, constraints, and relationships. Special attention is given to self-referential relationships that support hierarchical product structures.

## product

Stores all manufactured items including finished goods, assemblies, and components. Supports hierarchical product relationships via self-referencing foreign key.

| Column | Data Type | Constraints | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| product_id | VARCHAR(20) | PRIMARY KEY | Unique product identifier |
| product_name | VARCHAR(100) | NOT NULL | Product name |
| product_type | product_type_t | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 'FINISHED_GOOD' | Product classification: FINISHED_GOOD, ASSEMBLY, COMPONENT | | parent_product_id | VARCHAR(20) | FOREIGN KEY → product(product_id) | Parent product for hierarchical assemblies | | base_cost | DECIMAL(10,2) | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 0.00, CHECK >= 0 | Base manufacturing cost | | lead_time_days | INT | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 0, CHECK >= 0 | Procurement or production lead time | | is_active | yes_no_t | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 'Y' | Active status flag | **Indexes:** - `idx_product_parent` on parent_product_id (WHERE NOT NULL) — supports hierarchical queries **Self-Referential Relationship:** - `parent_product_id` references `product_id` — enables multi-level product assemblies - A product can contain other products as components via BOM - Supports unlimited nesting depth for complex assemblies ## component Stores atomic raw materials and purchased parts that are procured from external suppliers. | Column | Data Type | Constraints | Description | |--------|-----------|-------------|-------------| | component_id | VARCHAR(20) | PRIMARY KEY | Unique component identifier | | component_name | VARCHAR(100) | NOT NULL | Component name | | component_category | VARCHAR(50) | | Component category classification | | unit_of_measure | VARCHAR(10) | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 'EA' | Unit of measure (EA, KG, M, L, etc.) | | standard_cost | DECIMAL(10,2) | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 0.00, CHECK >= 0 | Standard procurement cost | | specification_id | VARCHAR(20) | | Technical specification reference | **Relationships:** - Many-to-many with supplier via component_supplier junction table - One-to-many with inventory - Optional relationship with specification

PRODUCT.PRODUCT_ID (VARCHAR, NOT NULL) — Product ID. Column in table PRODUCT: Stores information about products, including their identity, name, category, hierarchical parent product relationships, cost, production or delivery lead time, and active status.

Product: Product is the manufactured item in the system (finished goods, assemblies, and components) with a unique ID, name, type classification, base cost, and lead time, and participates in BOMs and production processes.

BATCH.QUANTITY_PRODUCED (DECIMAL, NOT NULL) — Quantity Produced. Column in table BATCH: Stores information about production batches, including which product and warehouse they belong to, production and expiry dates, quantity produced, and current quality control status.

Work Order: A Work Order is a manufacturing instruction to plan and execute production of a specified quantity of a product, tracking its lifecycle from planned through completed and supporting hierarchical relationships such as a parent work order.
Specification: A Specification is a formal document or set of technical requirements, standards, and acceptance criteria used to define and verify materials, components, or products.

Batch: A Batch is a production lot representing all units produced together in a single manufacturing run, with associated attributes such as production date, quantity, warehouse location, expiry date (if applicable), and quality control status.
Component: A Component is a discrete item used as a building block in manufacturing, such as atomic raw materials or purchased parts, procured from external suppliers.

## Warehouse

A Warehouse represents a physical storage location where materials, components, and finished goods are stored. Warehouses have defined capacity limits, geographic location information, and are managed by designated managers. Warehouses serve as nodes in the supply chain network where inventory is held, shipments are received, and products are staged for production or distribution.

**Key Attributes: ** - Unique warehouse identifier - Warehouse name and location - Address, city, and state information - Storage capacity limits - Manager assignment **Relationships:** - Warehouses contain multiple Inventory records - Warehouses receive and ship Shipments - Warehouses are linked to Work Orders for production staging - Warehouses store produced Batches ## Inventory Inventory represents the actual stock levels of components and products stored in specific warehouses. Each inventory record tracks the quantity on hand, quantity reserved for pending orders, and reorder thresholds to trigger procurement. Inventory records support either components or products (but not both in the same record) and include timestamps for the last restock date. **Key Attributes:** - Unique inventory identifier - Warehouse location reference - Component OR Product reference (mutually exclusive) - Quantity on hand (available stock) - Quantity reserved (allocated but not shipped) - Reorder threshold for replenishment - Last restock date **Relationships:** - Inventory belongs to a Warehouse - Inventory tracks either a Component or Product - Inventory levels trigger procurement decisions - Inventory is reserved for Work Orders and Shipments ## WorkOrder A Work Order represents a production job or manufacturing run to produce a specified quantity of a product. Work Orders track planned versus completed quantities, status through the production lifecycle, priority levels, and planned dates. Work Orders can have hierarchical relationships where a parent work order may decompose into child work orders for different production stages or sub-assemblies. **Key Attributes:** - Unique work order identifier - Product to be manufactured - Parent work order reference (for decomposition) - Quantity ordered and completed - Status: PENDING, IN_PROGRESS, COMPLETED, CLOSED - Priority: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, URGENT - Planned start and end dates - Warehouse location for production **Relationships:** - Work Orders produce Products - Work Orders can have child Work Orders - Work Orders are associated with Warehouses - Work Orders consume Inventory (components) - Work Orders produce output for Shipment ## Shipment A Shipment represents the physical transfer of materials between warehouses, from suppliers to warehouses (inbound), or from warehouses to customers (outbound). Shipments track shipment type, source and destination locations, dates for ship, estimated arrival, and actual delivery, along with current status. Internal shipments support material movement between warehouses. **Key Attributes:** - Unique shipment identifier - Type: INBOUND, OUTBOUND, INTERNAL - Warehouse destination or source - Supplier reference (for inbound) - Customer reference (for outbound) - Ship date, estimated arrival, actual arrival - Status: PENDING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED **Relationships:** - Shipments connect Warehouses, Suppliers, and Customers - Inbound Shipments reference Suppliers - Shipments update Inventory levels - Shipments fulfill Work Order outputs

per_question.question: What is the purpose of the Bill of Materials (BOM) table?
per_question.expected_answer: The BOM table defines the hierarchical structure of products by specifying which components or sub-assemblies are required to manufacture each parent product. It records the unique BOM entry identifier (bom_id), parent product being built (parent_product_id), component or sub-assembly product (component_product_id), required quantity per unit (quantity), unit of measure, depth level in the hierarchy (bom_level), and whether the component is optional (is_optional).
per_question.generated_answer:
The **Bill of Materials (BOM)** table’s purpose is to define the **composition and hierarchical structure** of products in the manufacturing supply chain.

Based on the schema and glossary, each BOM entry specifies a **parent product** (the item being built) and a **component product** (what goes into it), along with the **required quantity**, **unit of measure**, and the **BOM hierarchy level (bom_level)** to support **multi-level assemblies**. The table also marks whether a component is **optional** (via **is_optional** / **is_optional** flag). It supports recursive relationships so the BOM can represent complete product structures and enable **hierarchical product explosions** (used for material requirements planning).

## shipment

Tracks material transfers between warehouses, suppliers, and customers.

| Column | Data Type | Constraints | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| shipment_id | VARCHAR(20) | PRIMARY KEY | Unique shipment identifier |
| shipment_type | shipment_type_t | NOT NULL | INBOUND, OUTBOUND, INTERNAL |
| warehouse_id | VARCHAR(20) | FOREIGN KEY → warehouse(warehouse_id) | Warehouse reference |
| supplier_id | VARCHAR(20) | FOREIGN KEY → supplier(supplier_id) | Supplier (inbound only) |
| customer_id | VARCHAR(20) | | Customer reference (outbound) |
| ship_date | DATE | NOT NULL | Shipment date |
| estimated_arrival | DATE | | Estimated arrival date |
| actual_arrival | DATE | | Actual arrival date |
| status | shipment_status_t | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 'PENDING' | PENDING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED |

In the manufacturing system, work orders are structured around the **`work_order`** table, which represents production jobs for manufacturing products and supports hierarchical decomposition.

Each work order has a unique **`work_order_id`** and is tied to the item to be manufactured via **`product_id`** (a required foreign key to `product(product_id)`). Work orders can also be linked to a parent work order through **`parent_work_order`**, which is a foreign key back to **`work_order(work_order_id)`**. This self-referential relationship enables “a large production job” to be broken down into child work orders for different production stages or sub-assemblies.

Work order planning and tracking are handled with these core fields: **`quantity_ordered`** (required, default 0.00, must be > 0), **`quantity_completed`** (required, default 0.00, must be >= 0 and <= `quantity_ordered`), and a production lifecycle **`status`** (not null, default `'PENDING'`, with allowed values `PENDING`, `IN_PROGRESS`, `COMPLETED`, `CLOSED`). # Manufacturing Business Glossary

This glossary defines the core business concepts and terminology used in the manufacturing supply chain domain. It covers product structures, materials management, production workflows, and quality assurance processes.

## Product

A Product represents any manufactured item in the system, including finished goods ready for sale, sub-assemblies used in production, and individual components. Products are organized in a hierarchical structure where complex products can contain simpler products as parts. Each product has a unique identifier, name, type classification, base cost, and lead time for procurement or production. Products can be active or inactive based on current manufacturing requirements.

**Key Attributes: ** - Unique product identifier (20 characters) - Product name and description - Type classification: FINISHED_GOOD, ASSEMBLY, or COMPONENT - Hierarchical relationship via parent_product_id - Base cost and lead time information - Active status flag **Relationships:** - A Product can have multiple child Products (via BOM hierarchy) - A Product can reference a parent Product (for multi-level assemblies) - Products are used in Work Orders for production - Products appear in Shipments (outbound) - Products are stored in Inventory records ## Component A Component represents atomic raw materials or purchased parts that cannot be further broken down into simpler manufactured items. Components are the building blocks used in manufacturing and are procured from external suppliers. Each component has a standard cost, unit of measure, and may be associated with technical specifications that define quality requirements. **Key Attributes:** - Unique component identifier - Component name and category classification - Unit of measure (EA, KG, M, L, etc.) - Standard cost for procurement - Technical specification reference **Relationships:** - Components are supplied by multiple Suppliers (many-to-many) - Components have Inventory records tracking stock levels - Components may reference Specifications for quality requirements ## BOM (Bill of Materials) The Bill of Materials (BOM) defines the composition of products by specifying which components and sub-assemblies are required to manufacture a parent product. Each BOM entry indicates a parent product, a component product (which can be either a component or sub-assembly), the required quantity, unit of measure, and hierarchical level in the product structure. BOM entries support recursive relationships to represent multi-level product assemblies. **Key Attributes:** - Unique BOM identifier - Parent product reference (what is being built) - Component product reference (what goes into it) - Required quantity and unit of measure - BOM level (depth in hierarchy) - Optional component flag **Relationships:** - BOM connects Products to other Products (parent-child) - Multiple BOM entries define a complete product structure - BOM structure is used for material requirements planning - BOM levels support hierarchical product explosions ## Supplier A Supplier is an external vendor or company that provides raw materials, components, or finished goods to the manufacturing organization. Suppliers are evaluated based on performance ratings, preferred status for strategic partnerships, and maintain contact information for procurement operations. Suppliers are associated with components they can provide through approved supplier relationships. **Key Attributes:** - Unique supplier identifier - Supplier name and contact details - Contact email and phone number - Performance rating (1.0 to 5.0 scale) - Preferred supplier flag **Relationships:** - Suppliers provide multiple Components (many-to-many via component_supplier) - Suppliers are linked to Inbound Shipments - Suppliers have negotiated pricing and lead times per component

# Manufacturing Data Dictionary

This data dictionary documents the database schema for the manufacturing supply chain system. It includes all tables, columns, data types, constraints, and relationships. Special attention is given to self-referential relationships that support hierarchical product structures.

To determine the manufacturing route for a specific product, use the **`ROUTE`** table, which defines the production route as a sequence of operations tied to a product.

Filter **`ROUTE`** by **`ROUTE.product_id`** (this column is **NOT NULL** and is a foreign key to **`product(product_id)`**). The route steps for that product are then listed in operation order using **`ROUTE.sequence_number`**.

You should retrieve (at minimum) **`route_id`**, **`route_name`**, **`sequence_number`**, **`operation_name`**, and optionally the operational timing and location fields **`work_center`**, **`cycle_time_minutes`**, and **`setup_time_minutes`**.

Because **`uq_route_sequence`** enforces **unique (product_id, sequence_number)**, the ordering of steps per product is unambiguous, and you should sort by **`sequence_number`** to get the manufacturing workflow from first to last step.

Example (using a known product identifier):
```sql
SELECT
  route_id,
  route_name,
  sequence_number,
  operation_name,
  work_center,
  cycle_time_minutes,
  setup_time_minutes
FROM ROUTE
WHERE product_id = '<product_id>'
ORDER BY sequence_number;
```

per_question.expected_answer: Query the batch table filtering by warehouse_id. Join with the product table to get product names and types. per_question.generated_answer:
To find batches stored at a specific warehouse and their QC status, use the `BATCH` table’s warehouse link and quality field.

`BATCH` contains `WAREHOUSE_ID` (nullable) to indicate which warehouse the batch is stored in, and `QC_STATUS` (VARCHAR, NOT NULL) to indicate the current quality control status. The `warehouse` table provides the warehouse identity via `warehouse_id` (primary key) and `warehouse_name`.

Join `BATCH` to `warehouse` on `BATCH.warehouse_id = warehouse.warehouse_id`, then filter to the target warehouse, and select `BATCH.batch_id` plus `BATCH.qc_status`.

```sql
SELECT
  b.batch_id,
  b.qc_status,
  w.warehouse_name
FROM batch b
JOIN warehouse w
  ON b.warehouse_id = w.warehouse_id
WHERE w.warehouse_id = '<your_warehouse_id>';
```

This works because the QC status you need is stored directly on `BATCH` (`BATCH.QC_STATUS`), and the storage location is stored as `BATCH.WAREHOUSE_ID`.

Products are organized in a hierarchical structure where complex products can contain simpler products as parts. Each product has a unique identifier, name, type classification, base cost, and lead time for procurement or production. Components are the building blocks used in manufacturing and are procured from external suppliers. Each component has a standard cost, unit of measure, and may be associated with technical specifications that define quality requirements. **Key Attributes:** - Unique component identifier - Component name and category classification - Unit of measure (EA, KG, M, L, etc.) - Standard cost for procurement - Technical specification reference **Relationships:** - Components are supplied by multiple Suppliers (many-to-many) - Components have Inventory records tracking stock levels - Components may reference Specifications for quality requirements ## BOM (Bill of Materials) The Bill of Materials (BOM) defines the composition of products by specifying which components and sub-assemblies are required to manufacture a parent product. Each BOM entry indicates a parent product, a component product (which can be either a component or sub-assembly), the required quantity, unit of measure, and hierarchical level in the product structure. BOM entries support recursive relationships to represent multi-level product assemblies. **Key Attributes:** - Unique BOM identifier - Parent product reference (what is being built) - Component product reference (what goes into it) - Required quantity and unit of measure - BOM level (depth in hierarchy) - Optional component flag **Relationships:** - BOM connects Products to other Products (parent-child) - Multiple BOM entries define a complete product structure - BOM structure is used for material requirements planning - BOM levels support hierarchical product explosions ## Supplier A Supplier is an external vendor or company that provides raw materials, components, or finished goods to the manufacturing organization. Suppliers are evaluated based on performance ratings, preferred status for strategic partnerships, and maintain contact information for procurement operations. Suppliers are associated with components they can provide through approved supplier relationships. **Key Attributes:** - Unique supplier identifier - Supplier name and contact details - Contact email and phone number - Performance rating (1.0 to 5.0 scale) - Preferred supplier flag **Relationships:** - Suppliers provide multiple Components (many-to-many via component_supplier) - Suppliers are linked to Inbound Shipments - Suppliers have negotiated pricing and lead times per component

## QualityControl

Quality Control (QC) represents inspection and testing activities performed on materials, in-process production, or finished goods. QC records track the type of inspection (incoming, in-process, final), inspection date, inspector, test results (pass, fail, conditional), defect counts, and notes. QC activities are associated with specific production batches and may reference technical specifications being tested.

**Key Attributes: ** - Unique QC identifier - Batch reference (production lot being inspected) - Specification reference (requirements being tested) - QC date and inspector - Type: INCOMING, IN_PROCESS, FINAL - Result: PASS, FAIL, CONDITIONAL - Defect count and notes **Relationships:** - QC inspections are performed on Batches - QC tests against Specifications - QC results determine Batch status - QC failures trigger rework or scrap decisions ## Specification A Specification defines technical requirements, standards, and acceptance criteria for materials or products. Specifications include version numbers, effective dates, specification types (dimensional, material, performance), critical parameters to test, and acceptable value ranges with units of measure. Specifications ensure manufactured products meet engineering and customer requirements. **Key Attributes:** - Unique specification identifier - Specification name and version - Effective date - Type: DIMENSIONAL, MATERIAL, PERFORMANCE - Critical parameter name - Minimum and maximum acceptable values - Unit of measure for testing **Relationships:** - Specifications define requirements for Components - Specifications are used in Quality Control inspections - Specifications have version control for engineering changes ## Batch A Batch represents a specific production lot or run of a product, tracking all units produced together in a single manufacturing cycle. Batches enable traceability for quality issues and inventory management for time-sensitive or lot-controlled products. **Key Attributes:** - Unique batch identifier - Product reference - Production date - Quantity produced - Warehouse storage location - Expiry date (if applicable) - QC status: PENDING, PASSED, FAILED, QUARANTINED **Relationships:** - Batches produce a specific Product - Batches are stored in Warehouses - Batches undergo Quality Control inspections - Batches may have expiry dates for perishable goods - Batches enable traceability and recall management ## Route A Route defines the sequence of manufacturing operations or steps required to produce a product. per_question.question: How can I perform a complete BOM explosion to find all components required for a finished product?
per_question.expected_answer: Use a recursive query starting from the product_id of the finished good. At each level, join the bom table to find component_product_id entries. For each component, check if product_type is 'ASSEMBLY' or 'FINISHED_GOOD'. If so, recursively query bom again using that component_product_id as the new parent. If product_type is 'COMPONENT', this is a leaf node. Accumulate quantities by multiplying bom.quantity at each level. Continue until all leaf components are reached.

To do a complete BOM explosion for a finished product, use the fact that the **`bom`** table is self-referential: both **`bom.parent_product_id`** and **`bom.component_product_id`** reference **`product(product_id)`**, and the BOM “supports recursive relationships for multi-level assemblies.”

Start with the finished product (a **`product`** whose **`product_type`** is `FINISHED_GOOD`), then repeatedly follow this chain:

1) Find direct components: `bom.parent_product_id = finished_product_id`  
2) For each component found, find its own components: `bom.parent_product_id = previous_level.component_product_id`  
3) Continue until there are no more BOM rows for the current component product.

In the explosion result you typically carry forward a “cumulative quantity needed” by using the **`bom.quantity`** at each step (and the **`bom.unit_of_measure`** from each BOM row). Because **`bom.quantity`** is defined as the required quantity of that component within its parent, cumulative requirements are computed by rolling up through the hierarchy (i.e., multiplying quantities across levels).

If you want to exclude optional parts, filter on **`bom.is_optional`** (it is a required NOT NULL flag with default `'N'`).

Here is the structure of a recursive approach using only the schema concepts/columns provided (template style):

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE bom_explosion AS (
  -- Anchor: components directly under the finished product
  SELECT
    b.parent_product_id,
    b.component_product_id,
    b.bom_level,
    b.is_optional,
    CAST(b.quantity AS DECIMAL(38,10)) AS cumulative_quantity,
    b.unit_of_measure
  FROM bom b
  WHERE b.parent_product_id = :finished_product_id

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive: components under components (multi-level)
  SELECT
    be.parent_product_id,
    b.component_product_id,
    be.bom_level + 1 AS bom_level,
    b.is_optional,
    be.cumulative_quantity * b.quantity AS cumulative_quantity,
    b.unit_of_measure
  FROM bom_explosion be
  JOIN bom b
    ON b.parent_product_id = be.component_product_id
)
SELECT
  parent_product_id,
  component_product_id,
  SUM(cumulative_quantity) AS total_quantity_needed,
  unit_of_measure
FROM bom_explosion
WHERE is_optional = 'N'   -- optional: remove this predicate if you want optional components too
GROUP BY parent_product_id, component_product_id, unit_of_measure;
```

For performance and “complete” traversal, the schema provides:
- an index on **`bom.parent_product_id`** (**`idx_bom_parent`**) specifically described as supporting BOM explosion queries
- **`uq_bom_structure (parent_product_id, component_product_id)`** to prevent duplicate BOM entries for the same parent/component pair

per_question.question: How can I find all parent products that contain a specific component anywhere in their BOM structure?
per_question.expected_answer: Use a recursive reverse traversal of the BOM. Start with the component_id as the initial parent_product_id. Find all records where this product appears as component_product_id in the bom table. For each parent_product_id found, check if it appears as a component_product_id in other bom records (is a sub-assembly used in higher-level assemblies). Recursively ascend until reaching top-level products with no parent_product_id in the product table.
per_question.generated_answer:
Use the recursive structure in the **`bom`** table to walk the hierarchy “upward” from the target component.

In **`bom`**, a product’s composition is stored as rows where:
`parent_product_id` = the product being built, and `component_product_id` = a product used inside that parent (including multi-level assemblies). Both columns are `FOREIGN KEY → product(product_id)`. The table also stores `bom_level` to represent depth.

To find “all parent products that contain a specific component anywhere,” you need the transitive closure over these relationships:

Start with the direct parents: all rows in `bom` where `component_product_id = :component_product_id`, returning their `parent_product_id`. Then repeatedly find higher-level parents by treating any previously found `parent_product_id` as a component: look for additional rows where `component_product_id` equals one of the previously found parent products, and collect their `parent_product_id` values as well. Return distinct parent product IDs.

A typical way to do this is with a recursive CTE over `bom`:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE bom_walk AS (
  -- Direct parents that include the target component
  SELECT
    b.parent_product_id
  FROM bom b
  WHERE b.component_product_id = :component_product_id

  UNION ALL

  -- Higher-level parents that include any previously found parent as a component
  SELECT
    b2.parent_product_id
  FROM bom b2
  JOIN bom_walk bw
    ON b2.component_product_id = bw.parent_product_id
)
SELECT DISTINCT parent_product_id
FROM bom_walk;
```

This uses the `bom.parent_product_id` / `bom.component_product_id` self-referential links described in the BOM definition to traverse the multi-level hierarchy, so it finds parent products where the component appears at any depth in the BOM structure.

per_question.question: How do I identify work orders that require a specific component, considering nested sub-assemblies?
per_question.expected_answer: First, perform reverse BOM traversal to find all parent products that contain the target component at any level. Then query the work_order table where product_id is in the set of parent products found. This includes work orders for finished goods that contain the component indirectly through sub-assemblies.
per_question.generated_answer:
To identify work orders that require a specific component when the component may be nested inside sub-assemblies, use the BOM hierarchy (multi-level nesting) starting from each work order’s produced product.

A work order is tied to what it manufactures via `work_order.product_id` (FK to `product.product_id`). The BOM defines composition via `bom.parent_product_id` (the product being built) and `bom.component_product_id` (the product component included). Because `bom.parent_product_id` and `bom.component_product_id` both reference `product(product_id)`, you can repeatedly “explode” the BOM: treat each `bom.component_product_id` as a new `parent_product_id` and continue until there are no further BOM rows.

Practically, you:
1) Start with each candidate `work_order` using `work_order.product_id` as the initial BOM `parent_product_id`.
2) Traverse `bom` rows where `bom.parent_product_id` equals the current product, collecting every reachable `bom.component_product_id` across all levels.
3) Keep only those work orders where the collected `component_product_id` equals the specific component you want. If the “specific component” is represented as a product record, you can also validate it with `product.product_type = 'COMPONENT'`.

Join path/columns involved from the schema:
`work_order.product_id` → `bom.parent_product_id` → `bom.component_product_id` (repeat explosion by using the prior `bom.component_product_id` as the next `bom.parent_product_id`) and (optionally) `bom.component_product_id` → `product.product_id` to check `product.product_type`.

If you represent the target as a product id (e.g., `:target_component_product_id`), the logic looks like:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE bom_exploded AS (
  -- Anchor: start from each work order's top-level product
  SELECT
    wo.work_order_id,
    b.component_product_id AS exploded_product_id
  FROM work_order wo
  JOIN bom b
    ON b.parent_product_id = wo.product_id

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive step: explode nested sub-assemblies
  SELECT
    be.work_order_id,
    b.component_product_id AS exploded_product_id
  FROM bom_exploded be
  JOIN bom b
    ON b.parent_product_id = be.exploded_product_id
)
SELECT DISTINCT
  wo.work_order_id,
  wo.status,
  wo.priority,
  wo.planned_start_date,
  wo.planned_end_date
FROM work_order wo
JOIN bom_exploded be
  ON be.work_order_id = wo.work_order_id
WHERE be.exploded_product_id = :target_component_product_id;
```

This finds work orders whose BOM (including nested sub-assemblies) includes the target component product at any depth, because the BOM self-references products via `parent_product_id`/`component_product_id`.

per_question.question: How do I find all products that have a specific product as a sub-assembly at any level?
per_question.expected_answer: Perform a recursive search starting with the target product_id as the initial component_product_id in the bom table. For each parent_product_id found, check if that parent appears as a component in other bom entries. Continue recursively until reaching products that are not used as components elsewhere. The result is the complete list of products that incorporate the target sub-assembly.

To find all products that contain a specific product `:target_product_id` as a sub-assembly at **any level**, use the multi-level BOM hierarchy in the **`bom`** table. A direct “any level” search is to recursively climb from the target product’s direct parents to higher-level parents:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE ancestors AS (
  -- level 1: products that directly use the target as a component
  SELECT
    b.parent_product_id AS product_id
  FROM bom b
  WHERE b.component_product_id = :target_product_id

  UNION ALL

  -- higher levels: products that use the previously found parents as components
  SELECT
    b.parent_product_id AS product_id
  FROM bom b
  JOIN ancestors a
    ON b.component_product_id = a.product_id
)
SELECT DISTINCT product_id
FROM ancestors;
```

Join path/columns used: `bom.component_product_id` (the thing currently “inside”) → `bom.parent_product_id` (the assembly that contains it). Repeating that join walks the BOM tree to any depth.

per_question.question: How can I calculate the total lead time for a product including all sub-assembly lead times?
per_question.expected_answer: Perform a recursive BOM explosion to find all components and sub-assemblies. For each product_id in the hierarchy, retrieve lead_time_days from the product table. The total lead time is the maximum of all lead times in the hierarchy (representing the longest dependency chain), or optionally sum lead times for each level if manufacturing is sequential rather than parallel.
per_question.generated_answer:
You can calculate total lead time by recursively traversing the product hierarchy using the self-referential relationship in **PRODUCT**: `PRODUCT.parent_product_id` (a child’s parent) → `PRODUCT.product_id` (the parent product). Since **PRODUCT.lead_time_days** exists on every product row and the context states this supports “multi-level product assemblies” with “unlimited nesting depth,” you can sum `lead_time_days` across the root product and all descendant sub-assemblies/components.

Example (recursive CTE):

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE bom_tree AS (
  -- Root product
  SELECT
    p.product_id,
    p.lead_time_days
  FROM product p
  WHERE p.product_id = :root_product_id
    AND p.is_active = 'Y'          -- optional

  UNION ALL

  -- Descendants (sub-assemblies/components)
  SELECT
    c.product_id,
    c.lead_time_days
  FROM bom_tree bt
  JOIN product c
    ON c.parent_product_id = bt.product_id
  WHERE c.is_active = 'Y'          -- optional
)
SELECT
  SUM(lead_time_days) AS total_lead_time_days
FROM bom_tree;
```

This sums the lead times for the entire hierarchy beneath the specified product (including that product itself). The context does not include any BOM “quantity per parent” information, so this approach totals lead times per hierarchy node rather than weighting lead time by how many times a sub-assembly is used.

per_question.question: How do I generate a complete indented BOM report showing the hierarchical structure?
per_question.expected_answer: Use a recursive query on the bom table that tracks the current depth level. Start with the top-level product (parent_product_id where product has no parent_product_id). At each level, output component details indented by depth level. Include product_name, quantity, and unit_of_measure. Recursively process all component_product_id entries that are assemblies until reaching leaf components.
per_question.generated_answer:
To generate a complete indented BOM report, you need to “explode” the BOM hierarchy by recursively walking the **BOM** table’s parent→component relationships, and pull the readable names from **product**.

From the schema/context:

- **BOM** contains the structure with:
  - `parent_product_id` (NOT NULL, FK → `product.product_id`) = what is being built
  - `component_product_id` (NOT NULL, FK → `product.product_id`) = what goes into it
  - `quantity`, `unit_of_measure`
  - `bom_level` = depth in the hierarchy
  - `is_optional` = optional component flag
- Both `parent_product_id` and `component_product_id` reference `product.product_id`, which allows recursive multi-level assemblies (a component can itself appear as a parent in other BOM rows).
- **product** provides the human-readable `product_name`.

A recursive CTE is the most direct way to produce an indented report for a chosen root product (e.g., a finished good).

Example pattern (root defined by `:root_product_id`):

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE bom_tree AS (
  -- Root line: the product you want the full BOM for
  SELECT
    p.product_id,
    p.product_name,
    0 AS bom_level,
    CAST(NULL AS DECIMAL(10,3)) AS quantity,
    CAST(NULL AS VARCHAR(10)) AS unit_of_measure,
    CAST(NULL AS yes_no_t) AS is_optional
  FROM product p
  WHERE p.product_id = :root_product_id

  UNION ALL

  -- Components at the next level
  SELECT
    bt.product_id,
    pc.product_name,
    b.bom_level,
    b.quantity,
    b.unit_of_measure,
    b.is_optional
  FROM bom_tree bt
  JOIN bom b
    ON b.parent_product_id = bt.product_id
  JOIN product pc
    ON pc.product_id = b.component_product_id
)
SELECT
  product_name,
  bom_level,
  quantity,
  unit_of_measure,
  is_optional
FROM bom_tree
ORDER BY bom_level, product_name;
```

To make it “indented,” use the fact that **BOM.bom_level** already stores the depth in the hierarchy. In your final SELECT, format a display column like “spaces + product_name” based on `bom_level` (for example, prefixing `product_name` with indentation proportional to `bom_level`), while still returning `quantity`, `unit_of_measure`, and `is_optional` for each component row. The recursive join (`bom.parent_product_id = previous level’s product_id`) ensures the report includes all levels reachable through BOM recursion.

# Manufacturing Business Glossary

This glossary defines the core business concepts and terminology used in the manufacturing supply chain domain. It covers product structures, materials management, production workflows, and quality assurance processes.

## Product

A Product represents any manufactured item in the system, including finished goods ready for sale, sub-assemblies used in production, and individual components. Products are organized in a hierarchical structure where complex products can contain simpler products as parts. Each product has a unique identifier, name, type classification, base cost, and lead time for procurement or production. Products can be active or inactive based on current manufacturing requirements.

**Key Attributes: ** - Unique product identifier (20 characters) - Product name and description - Type classification: FINISHED_GOOD, ASSEMBLY, or COMPONENT - Hierarchical relationship via parent_product_id - Base cost and lead time information - Active status flag **Relationships:** - A Product can have multiple child Products (via BOM hierarchy) - A Product can reference a parent Product (for multi-level assemblies) - Products are used in Work Orders for production - Products appear in Shipments (outbound) - Products are stored in Inventory records ## Component A Component represents atomic raw materials or purchased parts that cannot be further broken down into simpler manufactured items. Components are the building blocks used in manufacturing and are procured from external suppliers. Each component has a standard cost, unit of measure, and may be associated with technical specifications that define quality requirements. **Key Attributes:** - Unique component identifier - Component name and category classification - Unit of measure (EA, KG, M, L, etc.) - Standard cost for procurement - Technical specification reference **Relationships:** - Components are supplied by multiple Suppliers (many-to-many) - Components have Inventory records tracking stock levels - Components may reference Specifications for quality requirements ## BOM (Bill of Materials) The Bill of Materials (BOM) defines the composition of products by specifying which components and sub-assemblies are required to manufacture a parent product. Each BOM entry indicates a parent product, a component product (which can be either a component or sub-assembly), the required quantity, unit of measure, and hierarchical level in the product structure. BOM entries support recursive relationships to represent multi-level product assemblies. **Key Attributes:** - Unique BOM identifier - Parent product reference (what is being built) - Component product reference (what goes into it) - Required quantity and unit of measure - BOM level (depth in hierarchy) - Optional component flag **Relationships:** - BOM connects Products to other Products (parent-child) - Multiple BOM entries define a complete product structure - BOM structure is used for material requirements planning - BOM levels support hierarchical product explosions ## Supplier A Supplier is an external vendor or company that provides raw materials, components, or finished goods to the manufacturing organization. Suppliers are evaluated based on performance ratings, preferred status for strategic partnerships, and maintain contact information for procurement operations. Suppliers are associated with components they can provide through approved supplier relationships. **Key Attributes:** - Unique supplier identifier - Supplier name and contact details - Contact email and phone number - Performance rating (1.0 to 5.0 scale) - Preferred supplier flag **Relationships:** - Suppliers provide multiple Components (many-to-many via component_supplier) - Suppliers are linked to Inbound Shipments - Suppliers have negotiated pricing and lead times per component

per_question.question: How can I find which components appear most frequently across all product BOMs?
per_question.expected_answer: Perform BOM explosion for all products and aggregate occurrences of each leaf-level component. Count how many distinct product hierarchies contain each component. Join with component table for component names. Order by frequency descending to identify common components that should be prioritized for inventory management.
per_question.generated_answer:
To find which components appear most frequently across all product BOMs, use the fact that each **`bom`** row records one component inclusion via **`bom.component_product_id`** (a NOT NULL foreign key to **`product.product_id`**) along with the required **`quantity`**. So “frequency” can be computed by counting how many BOM lines reference each **component product**.

Because components are a product classification in **`product.product_type`** (values include `COMPONENT`), you can restrict to true components by filtering `product.product_type = 'COMPONENT'`.

```sql
SELECT
  p.product_id   AS component_product_id,
  p.product_name AS component_name,
  COUNT(*)       AS usage_count
FROM bom b
JOIN product p
  ON p.product_id = b.component_product_id
WHERE p.product_type = 'COMPONENT'
GROUP BY p.product_id, p.product_name
ORDER BY usage_count DESC;
```

This counts the number of times each component product appears in the BOM structure across all parent products (i.e., the total number of BOM rows referencing that `component_product_id`). If you want to measure “how many different parent products use the component,” replace `COUNT(*)` with `COUNT(DISTINCT b.parent_product_id)`.

per_question.question: How do I detect circular references in the BOM structure to prevent infinite loops?
per_question.expected_answer: Use a recursive query with a cycle detection check. Track the path of visited product_ids at each recursion level. If a product_id appears twice in the same path, a circular reference exists. Alternatively, check if any bom record has parent_product_id = component_product_id (direct self-reference), or perform depth-limited traversal and flag paths exceeding a reasonable maximum depth.
per_question.generated_answer:
To detect circular references in the BOM (so recursive BOM traversal can’t loop forever), use the fact that **`bom`** is a recursive parent/component structure: **`bom.parent_product_id`** (what is being built) and **`bom.component_product_id`** (what goes into it) both reference **`product(product_id)`**. Because a **`component_product_id`** can also appear as a **`parent_product_id`** in other BOM rows, the BOM graph can contain cycles (e.g., A → B → C → A).

The practical detection method is to recursively traverse the BOM edges and track the set/path of **product IDs visited in the current traversal**. A circular reference exists when, during traversal from a starting parent, you reach a **`component_product_id`** that is already in the current path (meaning the same **`product_id`** appears again in its own descendant chain).

Concretely, build a recursive walk where the next step uses this join condition based on the BOM columns:
`bom.parent_product_id = <previous step’s component_product_id>`
and carry forward a path variable (or visited-set) of the product IDs encountered so far.

You should also guard recursion depth using **`bom.bom_level`** (it is the hierarchy depth and is constrained to `>= 0`), and leverage the existing indexes for traversal efficiency:
`idx_bom_parent` (on `parent_product_id`) for the recursive join, and `idx_bom_level` (on `bom_level`) if you filter/order by depth.

Important: the uniqueness constraint **`uq_bom_structure`** on `(parent_product_id, component_product_id)` only prevents duplicate BOM rows for the same parent/component pair; it does **not** prevent multi-step cycles (A → B → C → A), so cycle detection via path/visited tracking is still required.

In short: recursively traverse **`bom`** from `parent_product_id` through `component_product_id` while maintaining the visited product IDs for each traversal; flag a cycle when a product ID is encountered twice in the same path, and optionally stop/limit by **`bom_level`** to prevent runaway recursion.

per_question.expected_answer: For the work_order's product_id, perform recursive BOM explosion to get all required components with quantities. To check whether the inventory recorded in **`inventory`** is sufficient to cover a **`work_order`**, you can compare the work order’s remaining quantity to the total inventory quantity for that work order’s **`product_id`** aggregated across all warehouses.

In the schema you have:

From **`work_order`**:
`product_id`, `quantity_ordered`, `quantity_completed` (so the remaining quantity to fulfill is `quantity_ordered - quantity_completed`).

From **`inventory`**:
`product_id`, `warehouse_id`, `quantity_on_hand`, `quantity_reserved`.
Also, `inventory` enforces that each inventory row is either for a component (`component_id`) or for a product (`product_id`) via `chk_inventory_item` (exclusive).

A practical check (across all warehouses) is:

```sql
SELECT
  wo.work_order_id,
  wo.product_id,
  (wo.quantity_ordered - wo.quantity_completed) AS qty_remaining_needed,
  SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) AS total_qty_on_hand_across_warehouses,
  SUM(i.quantity_on_hand - i.quantity_reserved) AS total_net_available_across_warehouses
FROM work_order wo
JOIN inventory i
  ON i.product_id = wo.product_id
WHERE wo.work_order_id = :target_work_order_id
GROUP BY wo.work_order_id, wo.product_id, wo.quantity_ordered, wo.quantity_completed;
```

Then:
If `total_net_available_across_warehouses >= qty_remaining_needed`, you have sufficient inventory available (net of reservations). If you prefer the gross check, use `total_qty_on_hand_across_warehouses` instead.

per_question.question: How can I find alternative suppliers for components that are critical for multiple products?
per_question.expected_answer: Perform BOM explosion for all active products and count component usage frequency. Identify high-frequency components. Query component_supplier table to find all suppliers for these components. Filter to suppliers with rating >= 4.0 (high quality) and is_preferred = 'Y'. For each component, list alternative suppliers with their unit_price and lead_time_days. This enables supplier diversification for critical materials.
per_question.generated_answer:
To find alternative suppliers for components that are critical for multiple products, use the BOM to determine which **component products** are used by more than one **parent product**, then use the approved-supplier junction to list suppliers and exclude the preferred one.

A “critical component for multiple products” can be derived from **`bom`** as follows: a component is critical when its `component_product_id` appears in BOM entries for more than one distinct `parent_product_id`. Because BOM `component_product_id` can be either a component or a sub-assembly (it references `product(product_id)`), first filter to only true components using **`product.product_type` = 'COMPONENT'**. The BOM also supports hierarchical/multi-level structures via recursive relationships, but the direct “used by multiple parent products” logic is still based on the same `parent_product_id`/`component_product_id` pairing.

Then retrieve suppliers from **`component_supplier`**, which links components to approved suppliers and includes per-component preference plus lead time and price:
- Join from the critical component list to **`component_supplier`** on `component_supplier.component_id = critical_component_id` (where `critical_component_id` is the component’s `product_id` from `product`/`bom`).
- Join to **`supplier`** on `component_supplier.supplier_id = supplier.supplier_id` to get supplier details like `supplier_name` and `rating`.

Finally, identify “alternative suppliers” by excluding the preferred supplier for that component using **`component_supplier.is_preferred`**. The junction table defines `is_preferred` as “Preferred supplier for this component”.

Overall join path: **`bom` → `product` (to ensure `product_type='COMPONENT'`) → `component_supplier` → `supplier`**, with “alternative” filtered by `component_supplier.is_preferred = 'N'`.

In SQL terms, the logic is: compute critical components with `GROUP BY bom.component_product_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT bom.parent_product_id) > 1`, filter component ids to those whose `product.product_type='COMPONENT'`, then select suppliers via `component_supplier` and exclude preferred ones via `component_supplier.is_preferred='N'`.

# Manufacturing Business Glossary

This glossary defines the core business concepts and terminology used in the manufacturing supply chain domain. It covers product structures, materials management, production workflows, and quality assurance processes.

## Product

A Product represents any manufactured item in the system, including finished goods ready for sale, sub-assemblies used in production, and individual components. Products are organized in a hierarchical structure where complex products can contain simpler products as parts. Each product has a unique identifier, name, type classification, base cost, and lead time for procurement or production. Products can be active or inactive based on current manufacturing requirements.

**Key Attributes: ** - Unique product identifier (20 characters) - Product name and description - Type classification: FINISHED_GOOD, ASSEMBLY, or COMPONENT - Hierarchical relationship via parent_product_id - Base cost and lead time information - Active status flag **Relationships:** - A Product can have multiple child Products (via BOM hierarchy) - A Product can reference a parent Product (for multi-level assemblies) - Products are used in Work Orders for production - Products appear in Shipments (outbound) - Products are stored in Inventory records ## Component A Component represents atomic raw materials or purchased parts that cannot be further broken down into simpler manufactured items. Components are the building blocks used in manufacturing and are procured from external suppliers. Each component has a standard cost, unit of measure, and may be associated with technical specifications that define quality requirements. **Key Attributes:** - Unique component identifier - Component name and category classification - Unit of measure (EA, KG, M, L, etc.) - Standard cost for procurement - Technical specification reference **Relationships:** - Components are supplied by multiple Suppliers (many-to-many) - Components have Inventory records tracking stock levels - Components may reference Specifications for quality requirements ## BOM (Bill of Materials) The Bill of Materials (BOM) defines the composition of products by specifying which components and sub-assemblies are required to manufacture a parent product. Each BOM entry indicates a parent product, a component product (which can be either a component or sub-assembly), the required quantity, unit of measure, and hierarchical level in the product structure. BOM entries support recursive relationships to represent multi-level product assemblies. **Key Attributes:** - Unique BOM identifier - Parent product reference (what is being built) - Component product reference (what goes into it) - Required quantity and unit of measure - BOM level (depth in hierarchy) - Optional component flag **Relationships:** - BOM connects Products to other Products (parent-child) - Multiple BOM entries define a complete product structure - BOM structure is used for material requirements planning - BOM levels support hierarchical product explosions ## Supplier A Supplier is an external vendor or company that provides raw materials, components, or finished goods to the manufacturing organization. Suppliers are evaluated based on performance ratings, preferred status for strategic partnerships, and maintain contact information for procurement operations. Suppliers are associated with components they can provide through approved supplier relationships. **Key Attributes:** - Unique supplier identifier - Supplier name and contact details - Contact email and phone number - Performance rating (1.0 to 5.0 scale) - Preferred supplier flag **Relationships:** - Suppliers provide multiple Components (many-to-many via component_supplier) - Suppliers are linked to Inbound Shipments - Suppliers have negotiated pricing and lead times per component

## Warehouse

A Warehouse represents a physical storage location where materials, components, and finished goods are stored. Warehouses have defined capacity limits, geographic location information, and are managed by designated managers. Warehouses serve as nodes in the supply chain network where inventory is held, shipments are received, and products are staged for production or distribution.

**Key Attributes: ** - Unique warehouse identifier - Warehouse name and location - Address, city, and state information - Storage capacity limits - Manager assignment **Relationships:** - Warehouses contain multiple Inventory records - Warehouses receive and ship Shipments - Warehouses are linked to Work Orders for production staging - Warehouses store produced Batches ## Inventory Inventory represents the actual stock levels of components and products stored in specific warehouses. Each inventory record tracks the quantity on hand, quantity reserved for pending orders, and reorder thresholds to trigger procurement. Inventory records support either components or products (but not both in the same record) and include timestamps for the last restock date. **Key Attributes:** - Unique inventory identifier - Warehouse location reference - Component OR Product reference (mutually exclusive) - Quantity on hand (available stock) - Quantity reserved (allocated but not shipped) - Reorder threshold for replenishment - Last restock date **Relationships:** - Inventory belongs to a Warehouse - Inventory tracks either a Component or Product - Inventory levels trigger procurement decisions - Inventory is reserved for Work Orders and Shipments ## WorkOrder A Work Order represents a production job or manufacturing run to produce a specified quantity of a product. Work Orders track planned versus completed quantities, status through the production lifecycle, priority levels, and planned dates. Work Orders can have hierarchical relationships where a parent work order may decompose into child work orders for different production stages or sub-assemblies. **Key Attributes:** - Unique work order identifier - Product to be manufactured - Parent work order reference (for decomposition) - Quantity ordered and completed - Status: PENDING, IN_PROGRESS, COMPLETED, CLOSED - Priority: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, URGENT - Planned start and end dates - Warehouse location for production **Relationships:** - Work Orders produce Products - Work Orders can have child Work Orders - Work Orders are associated with Warehouses - Work Orders consume Inventory (components) - Work Orders produce output for Shipment ## Shipment A Shipment represents the physical transfer of materials between warehouses, from suppliers to warehouses (inbound), or from warehouses to customers (outbound). Shipments track shipment type, source and destination locations, dates for ship, estimated arrival, and actual delivery, along with current status. Internal shipments support material movement between warehouses. **Key Attributes:** - Unique shipment identifier - Type: INBOUND, OUTBOUND, INTERNAL - Warehouse destination or source - Supplier reference (for inbound) - Customer reference (for outbound) - Ship date, estimated arrival, actual arrival - Status: PENDING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED **Relationships:** - Shipments connect Warehouses, Suppliers, and Customers - Inbound Shipments reference Suppliers - Shipments update Inventory levels - Shipments fulfill Work Order outputs

One-to-many with shipment (inbound) - Many-to-many with component via component_supplier ## component_supplier Junction table linking components to their approved suppliers with pricing and lead time information. | Column | Data Type | Constraints | Description | |--------|-----------|-------------|-------------| | component_supplier_id | VARCHAR(20) | PRIMARY KEY | Unique relationship identifier | | component_id | VARCHAR(20) | NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY → component(component_id) | Component reference | | supplier_id | VARCHAR(20) | NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY → supplier(supplier_id) | Supplier reference | | is_preferred | yes_no_t | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 'N' | Preferred supplier for this component | | lead_time_days | INT | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 0 | Procurement lead time | | unit_price | DECIMAL(10,2) | NOT NULL, DEFAULT 0.00, CHECK >= 0 | Negotiated unit price | **Indexes:** -
